seoTitle: How to Make a Talking Photo Video in Canva
metaDescription: Learn how to turn a still photo into a talking video in Canva, from importing images to adding text-to-speech audio and exporting the final clip.
intro: Bringing a static image to life with a voice adds a new layer to storytelling, whether for a personal project or a business presentation. This guide covers importing your images into Canva, adding text-to-speech audio to create the talking effect, and exporting the finished video. It's aimed at content creators and marketers looking for a new way to engage an audience.

steps.heading: Import your images into Canva
steps.body: Bring the photo you want to animate into your Canva project as the starting point for the talking video.

steps.heading: Add text-to-speech for the talking effect
steps.body: Use Canva's text-to-speech functionality to give your photo a voice, entering the script you want it to say.

steps.heading: Export your finished video
steps.body: Choose the right format when exporting so your talking photo video is ready to share.

steps.heading: Apply best practices for engaging storytelling
steps.body: Keep the script concise and the pacing natural so the finished video holds viewer attention.

faq.question: How does a talking photo video work in Canva?
faq.answer: Canva uses text-to-speech technology to give a static image a voice, turning it into a talking video based on the script you provide.

faq.question: What formats can I export my talking photo video in?
faq.answer: The tutorial covers choosing the right export format so your finished video is ready to share on your intended platform.

faq.question: Who can benefit from making talking photo videos?
faq.answer: Content creators, business marketers, and anyone looking to enhance visual storytelling can use this feature for products, explainers, or testimonials.

faq.question: Is this feature beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, the tutorial is designed for those just starting out with Canva while also offering tips for experienced users refining their video-making process.
